summary: This lesson introduces 'hace + time' for expressing 'ago', teaches the preterite endings for 'you informal' (-aste/-iste) and 'they' (-aron/-ieron), and provides a full panoramic review of regular preterite conjugations for both -ar and -er/-ir verb groups.

teachings.summary: To express 'ago' in Spanish, use 'hace' (it makes) followed by a time expression. 'Two weeks ago' becomes 'hace dos semanas' (it makes two weeks).

examples.english: I saw him two weeks ago.
I responded to her seven weeks ago.

examples.spanish: Se lo respondí hace un mes.
examples.english: I responded it to her one month ago.

teachings.summary: The preterite endings for 'you informal' (tú) are -aste for -ar verbs and -iste for -er/-ir verbs.
teachings.rule: -ar verbs: -aste (hablaste). -er/-ir verbs: -iste (comiste, saliste). Both contain 's' and 't' which relate to the informal you.

examples.english: Did you go out last night?
Why didn't you wait for me?

teachings.notes: Both 's' and 't' in the endings relate to informal 'you' and help you remember the form.

teachings.summary: The preterite endings for 'they' (ellos/ellas/ustedes) are -aron for -ar verbs and -ieron for -er/-ir verbs.

Complete review of all preterite forms. For -ar verbs (hablar): hablé, habló, hablamos, hablaste, hablaron. For -er/-ir verbs (comer): comí, comió, comimos, comiste, comieron. The -er/-ir group has 'i' dominating (the inverse of the present tense where 'e' dominates).
teachings.rule: In the preterite, -er/-ir verbs have the /i/ sound everywhere except -ieron (where /e/ gets one appearance). This is the exact inverse of the present tense where /e/ dominates and /i/ appears only in -imos.

examples.english: I spoke, he spoke, we spoke, you spoke, they spoke.
I ate, he ate, we ate, you ate, they ate.

teachings.summary: Practice deriving verbs from -ción nouns: discriminación -> discriminar.
